category: Large Polyp Stony (LPS) Coral — solitary disc polyp; true Scolymia (Caribbean); premium hobby coral

nativeRange.habitat: Turbid, low-light reef environments including reef platforms, sandy channels between reefs, and muddy slopes; particularly common at moderate to deeper depths where light penetration is reduced; prefers areas where sediment accumulation is manageable and competition from other corals is lower

decorPreferences: at least 15 cm spacing from neighboring corals
stable positioning — avoid high-flow areas that tumble the coral
shade-tolerant positioning away from intense overhead lighting
live rock in vicinity for water quality

diet.notes: Derives nutrition primarily from symbiotic zooxanthellae (photosynthesis) and benefits from supplemental target-feeding. Weekly spot-feeding with meaty foods — mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, krill, chopped marine fish, or small chunks of seafood — is generally recommended for long-term health. Feeds at night when tentacles are extended; delivering food via pipette or turkey baster directly onto the oral disc in the evening tends to produce the best uptake. Avoid leaving uneaten food in the aquarium to prevent water quality issues.

goodWith: peaceful LPS corals placed at least 15 cm away
avoidWith: corals placed within sweeper tentacle range at night
aggressive LPS corals (hammer, torch, frogspawn) without generous spacing
predatory fish that may nip at the fleshy polyp (triggers, pufferfish, large wrasses)
high direct flow that prevents polyp expansion or damages tissue

summary: True Caribbean Scolymia sp. — a large, solitary LPS disc coral and premium hobby species. Best placed on the sand bed under low light and gentle flow with weekly meaty feedings. Intermediate care.
careNotes:
Scolymia sp. is one of the most iconic and sought-after large polyp stony (LPS) corals in the reef hobby. Its large, fleshy disc polyp — typically 5–10 cm in diameter — can expand significantly during the day, displaying vivid coloration from solid reds, greens, and oranges to rare multi-color 'Master Scoly' morphs with complex patterns. Premium specimens have historically sold for hundreds to several thousand dollars, reflecting their wild-collection-only status and rarity.

Taxonomic note — Scolymia vs. Homophyllia:
This entry covers the true Scolymia genus (family Mussidae), which comprises exclusively Caribbean Atlantic species: S. cubensis (Artichoke Coral), S. lacera (Atlantic Mushroom Coral), and S. wellsii (Solitary Disk Coral). The genus was erected by Haime in 1852.

For most of the aquarium hobby's history, both Caribbean and Indo-Pacific 'scoly' corals were lumped under the Scolymia trade name. Molecular phylogenetic work in the early 2010s — building on structural analysis of the distinctive blunted and hillocked skeletal septa of the Indo-Pacific species versus the finer, more cylindrical spines of true Atlantic Scolymia — revealed that these were distantly related. The Indo-Pacific species (including the popular 'Australian Scoly') were transferred to the resurrected genus Homophyllia (family Lobophylliidae) and are now formally known as Homophyllia australis and Homophyllia bowerbanki. This reclassification has been widely accepted by WoRMS and adopted by the scientific community.

Important distinction: true Caribbean Scolymia (this entry) remains in family Mussidae, while the Australian trade species (sw-coral-065: [australian-scolymia-coral]) is Homophyllia australis in family Lobophylliidae — distinct families, different genera, and different regions. In practical hobby terms, 'Scoly' and 'Scolymia' may refer to either, with the Australian Homophyllia being the far more commonly traded animal.

True Caribbean Scolymia are infrequently collected for the trade compared to their Australian counterparts and tend to be less available. Their natural habitat at 6–80 m depth (Wikipedia; S. cubensis recorded to 62 m in Bermuda) means they can be shade-tolerant and adapted to lower light levels than the Australian species, which typically inhabits shallower reefs.

Key care requirements:

• Lighting: Generally advised to keep under low PAR (30–100). As a sand-bed dweller from naturally turbid, moderate-to-deep reef habitats, this species tends to prefer reduced light intensity. Excessive light can cause bleaching. ReefChasers reports successful keeping at 75–125 PAR; AquariumSource suggests 100–150 PAR. A conservative lower range (30–100 PAR) is adopted here given the species' natural deep-water and turbid habitat, consistent with the species' ecology and long-term aquarium success reports.

• Flow: Low flow is strongly recommended. Direct or high-velocity flow can prevent full polyp expansion and damage tissue against skeletal septa. Gentle, indirect, turbulent flow — just enough to prevent detritus from settling on the polyp — is the standard guidance.

• Placement: Best placed on the sand bed or low-lying rubble in the lower third of the aquarium. This matches the species' natural habitat on reef slopes and sand channels, and allows the polyp to face upward for light distribution.

• Aggression: Has been reported to extend sweeper tentacles nightly to sting neighboring corals. Maintaining at least 15 cm clearance from neighboring corals is generally advised. The fleshy expanded disc can occupy significant lateral space when fully inflated.

• Feeding: Weekly spot-feeding with meaty foods is the standard recommendation. Feeding in the evening when tentacles are extended is preferred. AquariumSource suggests feeding 2–3 times per week for optimal growth; weekly is a conservative baseline.

• Fragging: Caribbean Scolymia frags very poorly and is almost exclusively wild-collected. This contributes to higher prices and means each specimen should be treated with care.

• Care level: Sources generally rate the genus as beginner-to-intermediate when focused on Australian Homophyllia. True Caribbean Scolymia are somewhat rarer, potentially more sensitive given their deep-water origins, and less commonly kept — intermediate is adopted here.

• S. cubensis IUCN status: Critically Endangered. Wild collection from Caribbean waters has conservation implications; captive propagation, while difficult, is preferable.
breedingNotes: Sexual reproduction in the wild occurs via broadcast spawning, releasing eggs and sperm into the water column. S. cubensis larvae have been documented to fuse with genetically different individuals, forming di- or tri-corallites. Captive fragging is generally not feasible due to the solitary single-polyp structure and the high risk of permanent damage. Commercial propagation is almost exclusively through wild collection, primarily from Caribbean waters.

primary.name: ReefChasers — Scolymia Coral Care Guide
primary.notes: Used as primary source after LiveAquaria lacked a current Scolymia product page. ReefChasers provided: temperature 77–84°F (25–29°C), pH 8.0–8.4, kH 8–9, calcium 400–450 ppm, PAR 75–125, flow low-moderate, placement bottom, care level beginner, adult size ~4 inches, spot-feeding, sweeper tentacles at night (3 inch minimum spacing). Note: ReefChasers' guide covers both Homophyllia australis and trade-name Scolymia together; parameters are representative of the aquarium trade's 'Scoly' husbandry broadly.

additional.name: AquariumSource — Scoly Coral Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked: temperature 73–84°F (23–29°C), pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.025, kH 8–12, calcium 400 ppm, magnesium 1350 ppm, PAR 100–150, adult size 3–4 inches (7.6–10 cm). Feeding: 2–3 times per week. Minimum tank: 10 gal (understated; 150L adopted per playbook conservative rounding). Taxonomy listed as Mussidae/Scolymia with australis and vitiensis (now Homophyllia) — outdated but confirms Mussidae family reference for true Scolymia.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Scolymia (genus)
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Family Mussidae, Order Scleractinia), native range (Atlantic coast of the Americas — Florida, Caribbean, Gulf of Mexico, south to Brazil), current species (S. cubensis, S. lacera, S. wellsii), reclassification history (Cynarina lacrymalis formerly Scolymia lacrymalis; Homophyllia australis formerly Scolymia australis; Lobophyllia vitiensis formerly Scolymia vitiensis), depth range (10–80 m), sediment-shifting abilities, ecology.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Scolymia cubensis
additional.notes: Used for native range detail (Bermuda, Jamaica), depth range (6–62 m in Bermuda), maximum polyp diameter (10 cm), IUCN Critically Endangered status, reproduction biology (broadcast spawning, larval fusion to form di/tri-corallites), sediment management (mucus strands and mouth undulation), and low-light habitat preference. Family Mussidae confirmed.
